sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Bobby, presents with a 2-3 week history of right shoulder pain, initially developed after a period of intense physical activity in his backyard, including lifting and manual labor.
He reports the pain is located on the outside of the shoulder and is exacerbated by movement.
He has a history of similar shoulder pain from a past injury playing baseball, but this current pain is distinct from that.
He denies numbness in his arm or hands.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of shoulder pain from a previous injury playing baseball.
Review of systems: The patient reports soreness in his feet and ankles, but denies any numbness.
Current medications: I prescribed Advil for pain management.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are within normal limits: blood pressure 116/84, pulse 62, respirations 16, oxygen saturation 99%, and temperature 97.8°F.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
There is no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
The patient has a good hand grip, and on neurovascular exam, capillary refill is less than 3 seconds and sensation to touch is intact.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, an injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; unknown status for foot and ankle pain.
Plan:
I referred the patient to physical therapy for approximately 6-8 weeks to address the rotator cuff tendinopathy.
I recommended continuing Tylenol for pain management and suggested an MRI to further evaluate the shoulder.
If symptoms do not improve, a steroid injection may be considered.
I also advised the patient to try soaking his feet in Epsom salts to alleviate foot and ankle soreness.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in 2-3 weeks to reassess his symptoms and progress.
